back:
Fully managed service to deploy a web app or API directly from source code or a container image, with automatic build, deploy, load balancing, and scaling — no VPC/cluster/orchestration setup required.

Exam distinction from Elastic Beanstalk/ECS: App Runner is the simplest, most opinionated option, ideal for a single containerized web service without needing fine-grained infrastructure control.

Fully managed batch job scheduler that dynamically provisions the optimal compute (EC2, Spot, or Fargate) to run queued containerized jobs, handling retries and dependency ordering.

Exam trigger: 'run large numbers of batch computing jobs (simulations, ML training, nightly processing) without managing servers' — Batch handles queuing/scheduling that you'd otherwise have to build yourself on top of raw ECS/EC2.
Core IaaS compute, with the broadest range of instance families/processors (Intel, AMD, Arm) and purchase models to match workload cost, performance, and availability needs.

Automatically adds/removes EC2 instances based on scaling policies: target tracking (maintain a metric like CPU at X%), step scaling (react to CloudWatch alarm breaches), scheduled scaling (known traffic patterns), and predictive scaling (ML-based forecasting).

Also handles fleet health — replacing unhealthy instances automatically.

Exam nuance: combining an ASG across multiple AZs (and using warm pools to pre-initialize instances) is a common high-availability + fast-scaling pattern.

PaaS: upload code and Beanstalk provisions and manages the underlying EC2, ASG, ELB, and RDS resources, while still giving you access to the underlying resources if needed (unlike Lambda/App Runner which fully abstract them).

Exam distinction: Beanstalk is for teams who want managed infrastructure but still need some control/visibility into servers — a middle ground between raw EC2 and fully serverless options.

Serverless compute engine for containers — works with both ECS and EKS, removing the need to provision, patch, or scale EC2 worker nodes.

You pay per vCPU/memory reserved by the task/pod.

Event-driven serverless compute; max execution timeout is 15 minutes, memory configurable 128MB–10,240MB (with proportional vCPU), and ephemeral /tmp storage up to 10GB.

Cold starts and concurrency limits (default account concurrency limit, reserved/provisioned concurrency to control it) are frequent exam topics.

Simplified VPS offering with bundled compute, storage, and networking at flat monthly pricing — aimed at simple websites/apps and users who don't need the full flexibility (or complexity) of EC2/VPC.

Exam signal: 'simplest, cheapest option for a small, predictable workload with minimal AWS expertise needed' points to Lightsail rather than EC2.

Extends native AWS infrastructure (compute, storage, select services, and control plane) into an on-premises data center, fully managed by AWS on customer-owned or Outposts-form-factor hardware.

Exam trigger: low-latency access to on-prem systems, local data processing/residency requirements, or migrating apps with on-prem interdependencies that can't yet move to the cloud — distinct from Local Zones (AWS-owned infrastructure closer to users) and Wavelength (telco 5G edge).

Managed, private Docker/OCI container image registry integrated with IAM for access control, with vulnerability scanning built in.

It's the registry component only — pairs with ECS/EKS/Fargate for actually running containers.

AWS-native container orchestrator (simpler than Kubernetes) supporting two launch types: EC2 (you manage the cluster instances) or Fargate (serverless).

Task definitions describe containers, and services keep a desired count of tasks running behind a load balancer.

Extends ECS control plane to manage container workloads running on your own on-premises servers or VMs (external instances) alongside cloud-based ECS tasks, using the standard ECS APIs.

Niche but exam-testable for hybrid container management scenarios that need a single ECS control plane spanning on-prem and cloud.

Fully managed Kubernetes control plane (etcd, API server managed by AWS) with worker nodes on EC2, Fargate, or Outposts/on-prem via EKS Anywhere/Distro.

Choose over ECS when the workload requires Kubernetes-native APIs, Helm charts, existing K8s manifests, or portability across cloud providers — a very common SAP-C02 discriminator question.

Deployment option to run EKS's Kubernetes distribution entirely on your own on-premises infrastructure (bare metal, VMware), independent of an AWS Region connection — for air-gapped or fully on-prem Kubernetes needs while still using the same tooling as cloud EKS.

The open-source Kubernetes distribution that EKS itself is built on; you can download and run it yourself, decoupled from the managed EKS service, if you want AWS's tested/patched Kubernetes builds without using AWS's control plane.

front: What are the EC2 purchasing options?
back: On-Demand (no commitment, highest cost), Reserved/Savings Plans (1-3yr commitment for a discount), Spot (up to 90% off, reclaimable with a 2-minute warning — ideal for fault-tolerant/stateless workloads), and Dedicated Hosts/Instances (physical isolation, for licensing or compliance needs).

front: When do you pick Fargate vs. EC2-backed ECS/EKS?
back:
Pick Fargate when a question emphasizes 'no server/cluster management' for containers.

Pick EC2-backed when you need GPU instances, specific instance types, or tighter cost control at high, steady scale.

front: When do you choose Lambda vs. Fargate/EC2?
back:
Choose Lambda for short, event-driven work.

Move to Fargate/EC2 when a workload exceeds Lambda's 15-minute max execution timeout or needs persistent state between invocations.

front: When do you choose ECS vs. EKS?
back:
Choose ECS for simpler orchestration needs without Kubernetes-specific tooling/portability requirements.

Choose EKS when Kubernetes-native APIs, existing K8s manifests, or multi-cloud portability are required.
